title: 10 best herbal teas for high cholesterol
intro: Many herbal teas, such as garlic, artichoke, and turmeric tea, have a proven record in reducing high cholesterol levels. These teas contain potent antioxidants that help lower LDL (bad) cholesterol, while raising HDL (good) cholesterol. Moreover, hawthorn and psyllium teas exhibit similar benefits. Regularly drinking at least one of these teas daily contributes to improved cardiovascular health, as part of a balanced lifestyle and diet.

teas.tea_desc: Ashwagandha tea reduces high cholesterol by decreasing the levels of bad cholesterol, also known as low-density lipoprotein (LDL), in the blood. It accomplishes this by increasing the size of lipoprotein particles, making them less likely to get oxidized and clog arteries. Regular consumption of ashwagandha tea enhances the liver's ability to remove LDL from the body, further promoting cardiovascular health. Additionally, it stimulates the production of high-density lipoprotein (HDL), the "good" cholesterol that helps eliminate LDL from the bloodstream. Consequently, ashwagandha tea's active compounds foster a healthier cholesterol balance, contributing to overall heart well-being.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the ashwagandha plant are used to make medicinal tea for high cholesterol due to their adaptogenic properties that help to balance and stabilize the body's systems, including reducing cholesterol levels.

Leaves: The leaves of the ashwagandha plant are used in medicinal tea for high cholesterol as they contain steroidal lactones that have a hypolipidemic effect, reducing the levels of fats, including cholesterol, in the blood.

Seeds: The seeds of the ashwagandha plant are used in medicinal tea for high cholesterol due to their ability to lower cholesterol levels and improve the lipid profile, reducing the risk of cardiovascular diseases.
tea_constituents: Withanolides: These compounds in ashwagandha help reduce high cholesterol by inhibiting the accumulation of cholesterol in the body.
Sitoindosides: These constituents aid in managing high cholesterol by activating the liver's ability to remove cholesterol from blood circulation.
Alkaloids: Ashwagandha alkaloids help reduce high cholesterol by improving the metabolism of lipids in the body.
Steroidal lactones: These compounds aid in reducing high cholesterol by reducing the levels of bad cholesterol and increasing good cholesterol.
tea_recipe: In a pot, boil 2 cups of water for 5 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried ashwagandha root and reduce heat to a simmer.
Let the tea steep for 10 minutes, then strain out the solids.
Stir in 1 tablespoon of honey and 1/4 teaspoon of cinnamon powder.
Drink the tea while warm, once or twice daily, for best results.

teas.tea_desc: Guggul tea, derived from the resin of the Commiphora mukul tree, contains guggulsterones known to interfere with cholesterol absorption and production in the body. By decreasing cholesterol synthesis in the liver and increasing its elimination, guggulsterones promote healthy cholesterol levels. Research studies back this mechanism, providing evidence of its lipid-lowering effects. Furthermore, guggul tea activates receptors that aid in the removal of cholesterol from the bloodstream. Consequently, regular consumption of guggul tea helps maintain balanced cholesterol levels and supports cardiovascular health.
tea_parts: Guggul Resin: Obtained from the bark of the tree, it contains substances that help lower cholesterol levels.
Guggul Seeds: Rich in active compounds, these seeds help reduce high cholesterol by improving liver function and promoting the excretion of cholesterol-containing bile.
Guggul Leaves: Containing numerous beneficial polyphenols, these leaves help lower cholesterol by inhibiting its absorption in the intestines.
tea_constituents: Guggulsterones: These are plant steroids that help lower cholesterol levels by increasing the breakdown and removal of low-density lipoprotein (LDL), or "bad" cholesterol, from the bloodstream.
Z-guggulsterone: This compound inhibits the production of new cholesterol in the liver, reducing overall cholesterol levels.
E-guggulsterone: This compound helps reduce the oxidation of LDL cholesterol, preventing the formation of plaque in the arteries.
Guggulipid: This resin extract from the guggul tree contains a mix of guggulsterones that have been shown to lower total and LDL cholesterol, while increasing HDL "good" cholesterol.
Polyphenols: These antioxidants help prevent the oxidation of LDL cholesterol, reducing the risk of plaque formation in the arteries.
Flavonoids: These antioxidants help reduce inflammation and improve heart health, which can help lower cholesterol levels.
Triterpenes: These compounds have anti-inflammatory and cholesterol-lowering properties, which can help improve cardiovascular health.
tea_recipe: Measure out 1 teaspoon of guggul resin and 1 cup of water.
Grind the guggul resin into a powder using a mortar and pestle.
Bring the water to a boil, then mix in the ground guggul resin.
Let the mixture simmer for 10 minutes, then strain out the solids.
Consume the strained tea while hot, 2-3 times a day after meals.

teas.tea_desc: Turmeric tea contains curcumin, a compound recognized for its cholesterol-lowering properties. This compound reduces cholesterol levels by inhibiting the intestinal absorption of dietary cholesterol and promoting its excretion from the body. Moreover, curcumin stimulates the liver to produce less cholesterol, thus further contributing to reduced cholesterol levels. Additionally, turmeric tea prevents oxidation of LDL cholesterol, which is a critical step in the development of cardiovascular diseases. These combined effects make turmeric tea an effective tool for managing high cholesterol.
tea_parts: Rhizomes: The rhizomes of the turmeric tea plant are used to make medicinal tea for high cholesterol due to their anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce cholesterol levels.
Leaves: The leaves of the turmeric tea plant contain curcumin, a compound known for its ability to lower cholesterol levels, making it an effective ingredient for medicinal tea.
Roots: The roots of the turmeric tea plant are rich in curcumin and other compounds that can help reduce cholesterol levels, making it a commonly used part for medicinal tea.
Flowers: The flowers of the turmeric tea plant contain essential oils and antioxidants that can help lower cholesterol levels, making it a valuable addition to medicinal tea.
tea_constituents: Curcumin: Reduces inflammation and inhibits oxidative stress, helping to lower LDL cholesterol and prevent plaque formation.
Curcuminoids: Decrease cholesterol synthesis and increase HDL cholesterol, promoting a healthy lipid profile.
Essential oils: Stimulate bile production in the liver, enhancing the removal of cholesterol from the body.
Bisdemethoxycurcumin: Possesses anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, aiding in the prevention of cholesterol-induced atherosclerosis.
Demethoxycurcumin: Inhibits cholesterol uptake by intestinal cells, reducing the absorption of dietary cholesterol.
tea_recipe: In a saucepan, combine 4 cups of water, 1 teaspoon of ground turmeric, and 1/4 teaspoon of freshly ground black pepper. (Turmeric is fat-soluble, and black pepper enhances its absorption.)
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes to allow the turmeric to infuse.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup, pressing the solids to extract all liquid.
Stir in 1-2 teaspoons of honey (to taste) and 1 tablespoon of extra-virgin olive oil (for better absorption of turmeric) while the tea is still warm.
Serve immediately and enjoy daily for best results in managing high cholesterol levels.

teas.tea_desc: Ginger tea reduces high cholesterol by containing antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that actively lower the production of cholesterol in the liver. The primary active ingredient in ginger, gingerol, lowers LDL (bad) cholesterol and increases HDL (good) cholesterol levels. Moreover, ginger tea improves blood circulation, preventing cholesterol build-up in blood vessels and minimizing plaque formation. Additionally, ginger tea inhibits lipid peroxidation, linked to increased cholesterol levels in the body, helping to manage cholesterol levels and promote overall cardiovascular health.
tea_parts: Rhizomes: Ginger rhizomes are rich in antioxidants and have been shown to lower cholesterol levels by reducing serum and liver cholesterol.
Seeds: Ginger seeds contain active compounds that inhibit lipid peroxidation, which aids in maintaining healthy cholesterol levels.
Leaves: Ginger leaves are known for their cholesterol-lowering properties due to the presence of compounds that inhibit hepatic cholesterol biosynthesis.
tea_constituents: Gingerols: Gingerols are antioxidant compounds that help lower cholesterol levels by preventing the oxidation of low-density lipoprotein (LDL), or "bad" cholesterol.
Zingerone: Zingerone, a phenolic compound in ginger, helps reduce cholesterol absorption in the gut, thus lowering overall cholesterol levels in the body.
Shogaols: Shogaols are pungent compounds in ginger that inhibit the production of cholesterol in the liver, helping to maintain healthy cholesterol levels.
Paradols: Paradols, another group of antioxidants in ginger, contribute to reducing cholesterol by preventing plaque buildup in arteries and improving blood flow.
tea_recipe: Peel and grate 1-inch of fresh ginger; steep in 4 cups of boiling water for 10 minutes.
Stir in 1 teaspoon of honey and 1 tablespoon of lemon juice; mix thoroughly.
Add 1/4 teaspoon of cinnamon powder and 1/8 teaspoon of black pepper; blend well.
Strain the tea to remove ginger pieces; serve hot.
Sip slowly, allowing the tea to cool, to enjoy maximum benefits.

teas.tea_desc: Cinnamon tea aids in lowering high cholesterol due to its active compounds, particularly cinnamaldehyde, which inhibits the production of cholesterol in the body. This herbal tea also contains antioxidants that prevent the oxidation of low-density lipoprotein (LDL), or "bad" cholesterol, reducing the risk of plaque buildup in arteries. Furthermore, cinnamon stimulates the liver to increase high-density lipoprotein (HDL), or "good" cholesterol, levels, enhancing the body's natural cholesterol-cleaning process. Additionally, cinnamon tea promotes weight loss by regulating blood sugar levels and reducing insulin resistance, both of which contribute to lower cholesterol. These cholesterol-fighting benefits have been validated through numerous scientific studies, demonstrating the efficacy of cinnamon tea in managing high cholesterol.
tea_parts: Bark: The bark of the cinnamon tea plant is rich in antioxidants and contains compounds that help reduce the absorption of cholesterol in the stomach.
Stems: The stems of the cinnamon tea plant contain essential oils and other compounds that help lower levels of LDL (bad) cholesterol and increase HDL (good) cholesterol.
Leaves: The leaves of the cinnamon tea plant have anti-inflammatory properties and help lower triglyceride levels, which is beneficial for people with high cholesterol.
Seeds: The seeds of the cinnamon tea plant contain high levels of fiber, which helps reduce cholesterol absorption in the body and promotes overall heart health.
tea_constituents: Cinnamaldehyde: This compound in cinnamon is responsible for its distinctive taste and aroma. It inhibits the production of harmful cholesterol (LDL) while promoting the formation of beneficial cholesterol (HDL).
Eugenol: A potent antioxidant in cinnamon, eugenol helps reduce oxidative stress and lipid peroxidation, thus controlling cholesterol levels.
Epicatechin: A type of flavonoid, epicatechin aids in lowering cholesterol by preventing the oxidation of LDL and improving its clearance from the body.
Hydroxychalcone: A polyphenol in cinnamon, hydroxychalcone enhances insulin sensitivity, leading to better management of cholesterol levels.
Coumarin: Coumarin in cinnamon has potential lipid-lowering effects that can help maintain healthy cholesterol levels.
tea_recipe: Boil 2 cups of water in a teapot or saucepan for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 cinnamon stick (1-2 inches long) or 1 teaspoon of ground cinnamon to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let the mixture simmer for 10-15 minutes to release cinnamon's beneficial compounds.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the solid cinnamon remnants.
Stir in 1-2 teaspoons of honey (if desired) and drink the tea while it's still warm, up to 3 times a day.

teas.tea_desc: Cayenne tea reduces high cholesterol by improving blood circulation and promoting the breakdown of fats in the body due to its active ingredient, capsaicin. This compound lowers LDL (bad) cholesterol levels while increasing HDL (good) cholesterol levels, thus maintaining a healthy balance. Furthermore, cayenne tea inhibits the formation of cholesterol plaques in the arteries, preventing blockages and lowering blood pressure. Additionally, it helps to regulate lipid metabolism and boosts overall heart health, making it an ideal natural remedy for high cholesterol management.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in antioxidants, the leaves of cayenne tea plant are known to help reduce LDL (bad) cholesterol levels.
Seeds: Containing high levels of fiber, cayenne seeds aid in lowering cholesterol by preventing its absorption in the gut.
Fruits: High in capsaicin, cayenne fruits help in increasing the metabolism, thereby reducing the cholesterol levels.
Stems: Packed with essential nutrients and bioactive compounds, cayenne stems have a positive impact on maintaining healthy cholesterol levels.
tea_constituents: Capsaicin: Stimulates breakdown of fats in the blood, helping lower cholesterol levels.
Vitamin C: Reduces LDL ("bad") cholesterol, preventing plaque buildup in arteries.
Carotenoids: Inhibit oxidation of LDL cholesterol, reducing risk of heart disease.
Vitamin E: Slows formation of plaque in arteries, improving blood flow and reducing cholesterol.
Beta-carotene: Decreases LDL cholesterol levels and enhances HDL ("good") cholesterol.
Flavonoids: Suppress cholesterol absorption, lowering overall cholesterol in the body.
Essential oils: Increase bile production in the liver, aiding in cholesterol metabolism and excretion.
tea_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water, then steep 1 teaspoon of cayenne pepper in it for 10 minutes.
Add 1 tablespoon of raw honey and 1 tablespoon of lemon juice to the mixture.
Stir well to dissolve the honey, heating if necessary.
Strain the tea to remove pepper particles.
Drink the tea immediately for best results, up to 3 times a day.

teas.tea_desc: Ginseng tea contains active compounds, called ginsenosides, that possess cholesterol-lowering properties. These ginsenosides work by inhibiting the absorption of dietary cholesterol in the intestines and increasing the breakdown and excretion of cholesterol from the body. Moreover, ginseng tea demonstrates a regulatory effect on liver enzymes involved in cholesterol synthesis, further contributing to its cholesterol-lowering abilities. By reducing cholesterol levels in the bloodstream, ginseng tea helps promote cardiovascular health and diminish the risk of developing heart-related complications associated with high cholesterol. Independent scientific studies support these findings, reinforcing the potential of ginseng tea as a complementary approach for managing high cholesterol levels.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of ginseng tea plant contain beneficial compounds known as ginsenosides which help in lowering cholesterol levels in the body.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of ginseng tea plant are rich in antioxidants that aid in reducing bad cholesterol (LDL) and increasing good cholesterol (HDL) levels.
Leaves: The leaves of ginseng tea plant contain saponins, which have been shown to lower cholesterol levels in the blood.
Seeds: The seeds of ginseng tea plant are a good source of fatty acids, which have been found to have a positive effect on cholesterol levels.
Buds: The buds of ginseng tea plant contain active compounds like ginsenosides, which help in lowering cholesterol and preventing the development of atherosclerosis.
tea_constituents: Ginsenosides: These are natural compounds in ginseng that help lower cholesterol levels by reducing the absorption of cholesterol in the gut.
Polyphenols: These are antioxidants in ginseng that help lower cholesterol levels by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation in the body.
Saponins: These are compounds in ginseng that help lower cholesterol levels by preventing the absorption of cholesterol in the gut and increasing its excretion.
Sterols: These are compounds in ginseng that help lower cholesterol levels by inhibiting the synthesis of cholesterol in the body.
Flavonoids: These are antioxidants in ginseng that help lower cholesterol levels by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation in the body.
tea_recipe: Measure - Pour 1 cup of water into a pot and add 1 teaspoon of dried ginseng root or 1 ginseng tea bag.
Heat - Place the pot on a stove and bring the water to a boil over high heat.
Steep - Once boiling, reduce heat to low and let the ginseng steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain - Remove the ginseng and pour the tea into a cup, discarding the used ginseng.
Serve - Enjoy your ginseng tea up to three times a day after meals to help lower cholesterol.

teas.tea_desc: Green tea's catechins, specifically EGCG, inhibit the activity of an enzyme called COMT that breaks down beneficial epicatechins, thus increasing their availability to reduce LDL oxidation. Additionally, green tea catechins hinder the absorption of dietary cholesterol in the digestive tract. The polyphenols in green tea also stimulate the expression of LDL receptors in the liver, enhancing LDL clearance from the bloodstream. Moreover, green tea catechins demonstrate an inverse relationship with C-reactive protein (CRP), a marker of inflammation associated with elevated cholesterol levels. Consequently, regular green tea consumption contributes to lower LDL cholesterol, higher HDL cholesterol, and decreased risk of cardiovascular disease.
tea_parts: Leaves: Green tea leaves are rich in catechins, which are antioxidants that help reduce the absorption of cholesterol in the digestive tract.
Buds: Tea buds contain high levels of flavonoids, which have been shown to lower LDL cholesterol levels and increase HDL cholesterol levels.
Seeds: Green tea seeds, also known as tea nuts, contain vitamin E, which has been linked to a reduction in LDL cholesterol and an increase in HDL cholesterol.
tea_constituents: Catechins: These are antioxidants that help prevent the oxidation of LDL cholesterol, reducing the risk of plaque buildup in the arteries.
Epigallocatechin Gallate: This catechin is the most active and abundant in green tea, and it helps lower cholesterol levels by increasing the excretion of cholesterol in the liver.
Theaflavins: These polyphenols have been shown to lower LDL cholesterol and increase HDL cholesterol, promoting a healthier lipid profile.
Thearubigins: These polyphenols have been found to reduce LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels, and they may also improve the function of endothelial cells lining the blood vessels.
Caffeine: While not directly related to cholesterol metabolism, caffeine in green tea can enhance the absorption of catechins, leading to improved cholesterol-lowering effects.
tea_recipe: Boil 8 ounces of water, then let it cool for 2-3 minutes until it reaches a temperature of 160-170°F.
Place one tea bag or 1 teaspoon of loose green tea leaves in a cup or teapot.
Pour the hot water over the tea bag or leaves, ensuring they are fully submerged.
Steep the tea for 2-3 minutes to allow the flavors to infuse and release beneficial antioxidants.
Remove the tea bag or strain out the leaves before drinking the tea. Enjoy up to three cups daily for best results.

teas.tea_desc: Hawthorn tea reduces high cholesterol by containing antioxidant flavonoids, namely oligomeric procyanidins, which decrease LDL oxidation. This tea also has hypolipidemic properties, meaning it lowers lipid levels in the blood, thanks to the presence of plant sterols. Furthermore, hawthorn tea stimulates the excretion of bile, thereby aiding in the elimination of cholesterol. Additionally, it inhibits the absorption of dietary cholesterol in the intestines. Regular consumption of hawthorn tea helps maintain healthy cholesterol levels, contributing to overall cardiovascular health.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in antioxidants, the leaves of hawthorn tea plant help reduce oxidative stress and lower levels of LDL (bad) cholesterol.
Berries/Seeds: Containing high levels of pectin, hawthorn berries and seeds bind with fats in the intestines, lowering the overall absorption of dietary cholesterol.
Flowers: Packed with flavonoids, hawthorn flowers have been shown to dilate blood vessels, improve blood flow, and decrease levels of cholesterol in the blood.
Bark: Hawthorn bark has a high concentration of tannins, known to reduce LDL cholesterol by encouraging its excretion through the digestive system.
Rhyzomes: These horizontal underground stems contain active compounds that help lower cholesterol levels, improve blood circulation, and reduce the risk of plaque buildup in the arteries.
tea_constituents: Procyanidins: These polyphenols help reduce LDL cholesterol levels by inhibiting LDL oxidation and improving its clearance from the body.
Flavonoids: As antioxidants, flavonoids contribute to heart health by lowering inflammation, reducing LDL cholesterol, and preventing plaque formation.
Epicatechins: These flavanols help manage cholesterol levels by decreasing dietary cholesterol absorption and increasing HDL cholesterol concentration.
Chlorogenic acid: As a phenolic acid, it inhibits cholesterol absorption, lowers LDL cholesterol levels, and enhances bile acid excretion, reducing overall cholesterol.
tea_recipe: In a stove-top pot, combine 2 cups of water and 2 tablespoons of dried hawthorn berries.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 15 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the solid hawthorn berries.
Add honey to taste, if desired, and stir until fully dissolved.
Serve the hawthorn tea warm, drinking up to three cups per day to help manage high cholesterol.

teas.tea_desc: Holy basil tea contains ursolic acid and eugenol, compounds known to lower LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels. A study published in the Journal of Ethnopharmacology found that holy basil extract reduces total cholesterol and increases HDL cholesterol. This herbal tea also possesses antioxidant properties that prevent cholesterol from oxidizing and forming plaques in arteries. Furthermore, holy basil stimulates the liver to speed up cholesterol metabolism and elimination. Therefore, drinking holy basil tea regularly helps maintain healthy cholesterol levels and supports cardiovascular health.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in antioxidants, they are known to reduce oxidative stress and lower LDL cholesterol levels.
Flowers: Contain several beneficial compounds that inhibit lipid peroxidation, helping control cholesterol.
Seeds: Packed with nutrients and antioxidants that help balance cholesterol levels in the body.
Stems: High in rosmarinic acid, which helps improve lipid profiles and decrease cholesterol absorption.
Roots: Contain eugenol, a compound known for its anti-inflammatory properties that assist in lowering cholesterol levels.
tea_constituents: Ursolic acid: Reduces cholesterol levels by inhibiting the absorption of dietary cholesterol in the gut.
Ocimosin: Decreases cholesterol and triglyceride levels in the blood by increasing the metabolism of fats.
Rosmarinic acid: Inhibits the oxidation of low-density lipoprotein (LDL), preventing the formation of plaque in arteries.
Eugenol: Lowers cholesterol levels by activating a receptor that regulates fat metabolism and reduces inflammation in the cardiovascular system.
Carvacrol: Decreases cholesterol absorption in the intestines and stimulates bile production, aiding in fat digestion and elimination.
Linalool: Reduces cholesterol levels by activating a gene involved in cholesterol metabolism, increasing its conversion into bile acids.
Ursolic acid: Stimulates the conversion of cholesterol into pregnenolone, a precursor to steroid hormones, thereby reducing cholesterol levels.
Oleanolic acid: Decreases cholesterol levels by activating a receptor that promotes the excretion of cholesterol from the body.
Apigenin: Inhibits the synthesis of cholesterol in the liver, reducing overall cholesterol levels.
Cirsimaritin: Enhances the breakdown of cholesterol by increasing the activity of cholesterol esterase, an enzyme involved in cholesterol metabolism.
tea_recipe: In a teapot, place 1 tablespoon of dried holy basil leaves.
Pour 2 cups of boiling water over the leaves, allowing them to steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the used leaves.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
Consume the tea while warm, twice daily after meals for best results.

other_remedies: Among the most common and effective natural remedies for high cholesterol are soluble fiber, nuts, and plant sterols. Soluble fiber, found in foods like oats, barley, and certain fruits and vegetables, works by binding to cholesterol in the digestive system and carrying it out of the body. Nuts, such as almonds and walnuts, contain monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats that help lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels. Plant sterols, found in foods like vegetable oils, margarines, and fortified foods, block the absorption of cholesterol in the digestive system. By incorporating these natural remedies into a balanced diet, individuals can effectively manage their high cholesterol levels.
definition: High cholesterol is a condition characterized by excessive levels of cholesterol in the blood. This excess leads to the formation of plaques in the arteries, a process known as atherosclerosis. As a result, the flow of oxygen-rich blood to critical organs, such as the heart and brain, becomes restricted. Over time, this restriction results in an increased risk of cardiovascular diseases, including heart attacks and strokes. These conditions significantly impact people's lives, often leading to reduced mobility, cognitive impairment, and even death.
